coredata.dc:title: Ultrasound-assisted adsorption of chlorpyrifos from aqueous solutions using magnetic chitosan/graphene quantum dot‑iron oxide nanocomposite hydrogel beads in batch adsorption column and fixed bed
coredata.dc:creator: Barzegarzadeh, Mehdi
Amini-Fazl, Mohammad Sadegh
Sohrabi, Negin
coredata.dc:description: Chlorpyrifos is a hazardous material that pollutes the environment and also poses risks to human health. Thus, it is necessary to remove chlorpyrifos from aqueous media. In this study, chitosan-based hydrogel beads with different content of iron oxide-graphene quantum dots were synthesized and used for the ultrasonic-assisted removal of chlorpyrifos from wastewater. The results of batch adsorption experiments showed that among the hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites, the chitosan/graphene quantum dot‑iron oxide (10) indicated a higher adsorption efficiency of about 99.997 % at optimum conditions of the response surface method. Fitting the experimental equilibrium data to different models shows that the adsorption of chlorpyrifos is well described by the Jossens, Avrami, and double exponential models. Furthermore, for the first time, the study of the ultrasonic effect on the removal performance of chlorpyrifos showed that the ultrasonic-assisted removal of chlorpyrifos significantly reduces the equilibration time. It is expected that the ultrasonic-assisted removal strategy can be a new method to develop highly efficient adsorbents for rapid removal of pollutants in wastewater. Also, the results of the fixed bed adsorption column showed that the breakthrough time and exhausting time of chitosan/graphene quantum dot‑iron oxide (10) were equal to 485 and 1099 min, respectively. And finally, the adsorption-desorption study showed the successful reuse of adsorbent for chlorpyrifos adsorption in seven runs without a significant decrease in adsorption efficiency. Therefore, it can be said that the adsorbent has a high economic and functional potential for industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Chlorpyrifos
Fixed-bed column

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determining the surface charge of the adsorbent
The effect of pH on the swelling of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites
The effect of GQD-Fe3O4 content on the swelling of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites
The measurement of zero point of charge (ZPC)
Effect of GQD-Fe3O4 content on removal performance
Effect of ultrasonic on removal performance
Removal of pesticides from real wastewater samples
Comparison of the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) nanocomposite with existing materials

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their sincere gratitude to the University of Tabriz for providing financial support to this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of CS, CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15); b XRD patterns of CS, Fe3O4, GQD-Fe3O4, CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15) adsorbents.
SEM images of CS (a,b), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5) (c,d), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) (e,f), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15) (g,h), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10)- chlorpyrifos (i,j) adsorbents.
EDS-mapping images for CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) (a), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10)- chlorpyrifos (b) nanocomposites.
VSM of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15) nanocomposites.
Effect of pH on the water absorbency of CS, CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15) nanocomposites.
Shows the effect of GQD-Fe3O4 contents on the swelling ratio of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites.
Pareto chart results for the operational parameters.
Shows actual profiles together with suggestions of the optimal condition for chlorpyrifos adsorption.
Determination of the pH of zero point of charge (pHZPC) for CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) and chlorpyrifos.
Equilibrium adsorption of CS, CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15).
The effects of ultrasonic on removal percentage.
Thermodynamic plot.
Adsorption and desorption graphs of pesticide by CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10).
Breakthrough curve of pesticide removal by CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10).
Removal efficiencies of agriculture wastewater and distilled water samples under optimum conditions.
Schematic illustration of the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposite.
Adsorption mechanisms of chlorpyrifos.
Provides a list of coded factor levels and independent variables.
The results of BET analysis of the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) nanocomposite.
FBAC parameters: C0 = 10 mg/L, Q = 0.3 mL/min.
Comparison of the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) nanocomposite with the reported adsorbents for chlorpyrifos removal.

row.entry: Magnetic graphene oxide and carboxymethyl cellulose (MGOC)
Aminoguanidine functionalized magnetic graphene oxide (AGu@ mGO(R))

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The absorption process was carried out without ultrasonic irradiation.

head.ce:title: Ultrasound-assisted adsorption of chlorpyrifos from aqueous solutions using magnetic chitosan/graphene quantum dot‑iron oxide nanocomposite hydrogel beads in batch adsorption column and fixed bed

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Polymer Material Research Laboratory, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Polymer Material Research Laboratory, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chlorpyrifos is a hazardous material that pollutes the environment and also poses risks to human health. Thus, it is necessary to remove chlorpyrifos from aqueous media. In this study, chitosan-based hydrogel beads with different content of iron oxide-graphene quantum dots were synthesized and used for the ultrasonic-assisted removal of chlorpyrifos from wastewater. The results of batch adsorption experiments showed that among the hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites, the chitosan/graphene quantum dot‑iron oxide (10) indicated a higher adsorption efficiency of about 99.997 % at optimum conditions of the response surface method. Fitting the experimental equilibrium data to different models shows that the adsorption of chlorpyrifos is well described by the Jossens, Avrami, and double exponential models. Furthermore, for the first time, the study of the ultrasonic effect on the removal performance of chlorpyrifos showed that the ultrasonic-assisted removal of chlorpyrifos significantly reduces the equilibration time. It is expected that the ultrasonic-assisted removal strategy can be a new method to develop highly efficient adsorbents for rapid removal of pollutants in wastewater. Also, the results of the fixed bed adsorption column showed that the breakthrough time and exhausting time of chitosan/graphene quantum dot‑iron oxide (10) were equal to 485 and 1099 min, respectively. And finally, the adsorption-desorption study showed the successful reuse of adsorbent for chlorpyrifos adsorption in seven runs without a significant decrease in adsorption efficiency. Therefore, it can be said that the adsorbent has a high economic and functional potential for industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, pesticides are used to increase the disease-free harvest and the longer shelf life of crops. However, <0.1 % of the pesticides are deposited on target pests, while >99.9 % of used pesticides move into the underground and surface water, and consequently, they have harmful effects on humans and animals [1–3]. Among various pesticides, organophosphorus pesticides are one an important category of insecticides that are frequently utilized in agriculture to control pests. Chlorpyrifos, a chlorinated organophosphate pesticide is extensively used in fruits and vegetables. Chlorpyrifos causes chronic and acute poisoning and is absorbed through the skin. Chlorpyrifos poisoning in humans may experience neurological, cardiovascular, and respiratory effects [4,5]. Various methods, including photocatalytic, advanced oxidation, electrochemical decomposition, membrane, and adsorption, have been investigated for the removal of pesticides. Commonly, some of the mentioned techniques are complex processes, which incur high capital and operating costs as well as higher chemical consumption [6–11]. In this context, scientists have discovered that the adsorption process is very effective for the removal of organophosphorus pesticides. But, there is constantly a problematic challenge to design an efficient, eco-friendly, low-cost, stable, and promising adsorbent in this method. Among the different materials utilized for the removal of pesticides, carbohydrate polymers such as starch, cellulose, lignin, alginate, and chitosan have received great interest as efficient and alternative adsorbents [12,13]. Chitosan hydrogel beads (CS), a promising biosorbent, have garnered a lot of interest because of their distinctive properties, including numerous functional groups, wide availability, affordability, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and biodegradability [14]. However, the low mechanical strength and complicated recovery process of chitosan hydrogel beads restrict their application as an efficient adsorbent. Iron oxide has unique properties that make it an attractive nanoparticle for adsorption applications. By introducing Fe3O4 nanoparticles on chitosan hydrogel beads, the chemical stability of bare CS is improved, and also magnetic property is created, which contributes to better separation [15,16]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a member of carbon-based nanomaterials smaller than 10 nm possess unique properties, including numerous hydroxyl groups, chemical inertness, marvelous bio consistency, high surface properties, and excellent thermal and mechanical stability. The importance of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in adsorption has been recently evaluated. Therefore, discovering the significant potential of graphene quantum dots in removing pesticides and enhancing the features of various adsorbents such as hydrogel, MOF, aerogel, and hydrogel beads can be a precious achievement [17–19].

As a rapidly growing field of research, ultrasound induces cavitation that increases the mass transfer rate near the surface [20]. In this technique, the propagation of a pressure wave in a liquid causes the formation, growth, and short collapse of tiny gas bubbles, which is an effective instrument for increasing mass transfer and breaking the affinity between adsorbent and adsorbate. Ultrasonic has been demonstrated to be a useful device in accelerating the adsorption of pollutants by increasing effectual interparticle collisions [21,22].

In this paper, we have developed an effective bead adsorbent material with low cost, high adsorption capacity, sufficient stability, and good separation efficiency in removing chlorpyrifos pesticide. Three types of chitosan-based hydrogel beads with different content of Fe3O4-graphene quantum dots (CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites (were synthesized (Scheme 1 ). In The first step, the effect of efficient parameters such as initial concentration, initial pH, contact time, and the number of beads was optimized by the response surface method (RSM). Also, the effect of ultrasound and GQD-Fe3O4 content on the removal efficiency was studied. Isotherm, thermodynamic, and kinetics experiments of chlorpyrifos pesticide adsorption onto CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposite were investigated by different models. In the second step, the performance of using CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposite as a strong adsorption media for the removal of chlorpyrifos pesticide from the water was investigated in fixed bed column studies. The breakthrough time (tb) and exhausting time (te) of hydrogel beads were calculated under optimal conditions of RSM.
Materials and experimental methods are expressed in detail in the Supplementary materials.

Graphene quantum dot (GQD) was first synthesized by the direct pyrolysis of citric acid. Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were prepared using the co-precipitation reaction of FeSO4.7H2O and FeCl3.6H2O in the presence of sodium hydroxide [23]. For the preparation of GQD–Fe3O4 nanoparticles, iron oxide (2 g) was ultrasonically dispersed in a 100 mL GQD solution (0.02 g/mL) for 25 min using a bath sonicator. The resulting mixture was stirred under a nitrogen atmosphere for 12 h. The precipitate was magnetically separated from the mixture, repeatedly washed with distilled water, and dried at 50° [24].

Hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites (CS/GQD-Fe3O4) with different weight ratios of 5, 10, and 15 % GQD-Fe3O4 were synthesized by the instantaneous gelation method. First, 40 mL of 2.5 % (v/v) aqueous acetic acid was used to dissolve 1 g of chitosan at room temperature, then a predetermined amount GQD-Fe3O4 was sonicated for 15 min for dispersion in the chitosan solution. Next, the uniform CS/GQD-Fe3O4 solution was introduced as droplets into a bath containing an alkaline mixture (H2O: NaOH = 9:1, w/w) to form CS/GQD-Fe3O4. The hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites were repeatedly washed with distilled water to neutralize the pH and dried at 40°. In this way, the amount of GQD-Fe3O4 added to each CS solution (40 ml) was 0.05, 0.1, and 0.15 g, and these hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites were coded as CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15), respectively.

Chlorpyrifos adsorption experiments on CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) were optimized by the response surface method (RSM). The adsorption study (adsorption efficiency) was carried out with different dosages of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) (0.5 g to 2.5 g), pH (3–11), initial pesticide concentrations (10–90 mg/L), and time (20–60 min). The Concentration of chlorpyrifos solutions after adsorption was analyzed by UV–Vis spectroscopy at their maximum adsorption wavelengths (302 nm). For this purpose, prepared the different solutions containing various concentrations of chlorpyrifos (0 to 200 mg/L), and the calibration curve and equation for chlorpyrifos were drawn and calculated. Then, the concentration of chlorpyrifos in unknown samples was obtained using a calibration equation. The pesticide removal (%R) and adsorbed amount of pesticide at equilibrium (qe) were calculated following Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), respectively.
(1)
% R =
C i
−
C e
C i
× 100
(2)
q e
=
C i
−
C e
×
V m
Here, qe is the quantity of adsorption at equilibrium (g/kg), R is the adsorption percentage, C0 and Ct are the initial concentrations and the final concentrations of the chlorpyrifos (ppm), respectively. m (g) is the weight of the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and V (mL) is the volume of the chlorpyrifos solution.

RSM allows the different tested conditions to be optimized and the interaction between several factors to be studied, to improve process performance. The variables that affect the adsorption process, including adsorbent dosages, pH, initial metal concentrations, and time at the five levels described, −α, −1, 0, 1, and +α with four replications at the central point, and 28 (
2 k
+2 k + 4 = 28) (k = number of effective variables) tests were performed (Table 1 ). The effects of each of the selected variables in the adsorption process were analyzed using Design Expert 11 software. Additional information about the parameters of the models used is accessible in the Supporting Information (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determining the surface charge of the adsorbent
ce:section.ce:para: The zero point charge (pHZPC) of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) and chlorpyrifos were determined by the pH shift method in order to investigate electrostatic interaction. To a series of vials, 10 mL of 0.01 M NaCl solution was added and the pH was adjusted by using HCl and NaOH in the pH range of 1–11. Next, 10 mg adsorbent and 1 mg adsorbate were added separately to the 10 mL solutions, and the resulting suspensions were equilibrated for 24 h. The final pH value was measured again, and the dissimilarities in pH were plotted against the starting value of pH.

Continues adsorption tests were performed in a fixed bed adsorption column (FBAC) [25,26]. In this system, a glass column was used with a diameter and length equal to 4 cm and 30 cm, respectively. In this column, to eliminate loss of adsorbent and restrict the wash away adsorbent was sandwiched between the layers of cotton wool. In a column, at the top and bottom of this, two layers of glass beads are used to prevent the channeling phenomenon, and the pesticide solution has a uniform distribution. The flow rate of the pesticide solution was regulated by a peristaltic pump and the soluble flow in the column is from the bottom to up. The best concentration of pesticide was selected based on previous studies and is equal to 1 ppm [27]. The pH and concentration of pesticide in all of the experiments in this section were adjusted to optimal, which was determined in the batch adsorption process. The output solutions from FBAC were sampled at various times following the adsorption process. These samples were analyzed to determine the changes in pesticide concentration. The time required to achieve the breakthrough (tb) and exhaustion (te) conditions of the column can be predicted using this data. In the FBAC experiment breakthrough curve was drawn by plotting the residual concentration of pesticide (Ct) versus time (t). A Breakthrough curve (BTC) was obtained by plotting Ct (concentration of pesticide in t, ppm) versus t (time of operation column, min). The time equivalent to total capacity (as the exhausting time, te) and time equivalent to usable capacity (as the breakthrough time, tb) are expressed by Eq. (3) and Eq. (4), respectively [28].
(3)
t e
=
∫
t = 0
t = total
1 −
C t
C 0
dt
(4)
t b
=
∫
t = 0
t b
1 −
C t
C 0
dt
In these equations, Ct and C0 are the pesticide concentrations of effluent (outlet filtrate) and influent (inlet feed), respectively. Also, breakthrough time is the time required for the concentration of pesticide in the effluent to achieve Ct/C0 = 0.05. During column operation when effluent concentration achieves 99 % of influent concentration (i.e., Ct/C0 = 0.99) is known as the exhaustion time (te) of FBAC [29]. The fixed bed adsorption column is almost exhausted at the exhaustion time stage (te). Therefore, in this research, the column ran until the exhaustion time (te) was reached. The adsorption capacity for adsorbent in FBAC (qeq), the total amount of adsorbed pesticide (qtotal), influent flow rate (Q, mL/min), and the equilibrium capacity of adsorbent for pesticide (qeq) in the FBAC at a given concentration (Ct) can be calculated by Eq. (5) [30]:
(5)
q total
=
Q × A
1000
=
Q ×
C 0
1000
∫
t = 0
t = total
1 −
C t
C 0
dt
q eq
=
q total
X
In this equation, X is the total mass of the adsorbent (g) and A is the area under the Breakthrough curve.

Eq. (6) can be used to estimate the mass transfer zone (MTZ), which is the length of the adsorption zone in the column.
(6)
MTZ = Z ×
t e
−
t b
t e
where Z is total bed height (cm), tb is breakthrough time (min), and te (min) is exhaustion time.

The empty bed contact time (EBCT) is a term used to describe the time of contact between the adsorbent sample and the adsorbate solution. It is a direct function of the volume of the adsorbent bed and influent flow rate, which follows from Eq. (7).
(7)
EBCT =
Z × S
Q
=
V B
Q
where VB (mL) and Q (mL/min) determine the bed volume and the influent flow rate, respectively.

The treated effluent volume(Veff) is designated through the expression given by Eq. (8):
(8)
V eff
= Q ×
t e
Also, the number of bed volume (NBV) and adsorbent exhaustion rate (AER) were calculated by Eqs. (9) and (10) [28]:
(9)
NBV =
Q ×
t b
S × Z
(10)
AER =
X
V eff

To calculate the adsorption percentage of Pesticide in FBAC Eq. (11) was used:
(11)
% A =
m ads
m total
× 100
In this equation m total is the total amount of Pesticide fed to the column and obtained by Eq. (12), and mads is the amount of Pesticide captured in the column and calculated by Eq. (13):
(12)
m total
=
C 0
× Q ×
t e
1000
(13)
m ads
=
Q ×
A 2
−
A 1
1000
In Eq. (13) A1 and A2 are the area on the Breakthrough curve that are calculated by Eqs. (14) and (15):
(14)
A 1
=
∫
t = 0
t =
t e
C t
dt
(15)
A 2
= 0.99 ×
C 0
×
t e

In this study, 0.1 M of sodium hydroxide solution was used as a desorption eluent in the desorption process. The desorption percentage of pesticide was determined using Eq. (16) [31]:
(16)
% of Desorption =
amount of pesticide desorbed amount of pesticide adsorbed
× 100

FT-IR spectra of CS, GQD-Fe3O4, CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15) are shown in Fig. 1a. The FT-IR spectrum of chitosan beads (CS) exhibited the following absorption bands at 1626 cm−1 (CO stretching of amide I), 1590 cm−1 (-NH2 bending) and 3434 cm−1 (–OH and –NH2 groups stretching vibration) [32,33]. For the GQD-Fe3O4, the peaks at 3452 and 1586 cm−1 are ascribed to the OH and CO peak stretching vibrations, respectively. The band at about 1390 cm−1 could be assigned to the symmetric stretching of the -CH2- group. The peak at 589 cm−1 is ascribed to the vibration of the FeO stretching band [34]. For CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites, as expected, there are significant changes between the FT-IR spectra of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites with GQD-Fe3O4 and CS, and the adsorption peaks related to chitosan were observed and strengthened, with a significant shift to higher wave numbers, indicating that different interactions such as intermolecular electrostatics may have occurred between CS and GQD-Fe3O4. The FT-IR spectra of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites prepared with 5, 10, and 15 percentages are very similar to each other, but some minor shifts and changes in the intensity of several vibrational bands of FeO, CH2, and CO, which shows that the nanocomposites hydrogel beads have a similar structure.

The XRD results of CS, Fe3O4, GQD-Fe3O4, CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15) are presented in Fig. 1b. All the prepared hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites exhibited two peaks at 2θ = 10.86° and 20.2° correspond to (020), (110), respectively, which conform to the structure of CS [35]. This result demonstrates that the structure of chitosan is unchanged by the addition of GQD-Fe3O4. The XRD diffractogram of Fe3O4 showed peaks at 30.56°, 35.84°, 43.46°, 54.12°, 57.44°, and 63.02° were assigned to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) reflections, respectively. The diffractogram of GQD-Fe3O4 is related to diffractions at 2θ values of about 26.06°, 30.2°, 35.66°, 42.84°, 57.3°, and 62.92°, which correspond to the crystal planes of (002), (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440), respectively [23]. All the diffraction peaks of GQD-Fe3O4 can be found in the pattern of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites, further indicating the successful preparation of the hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites. Additionally, with the rise of GQD-Fe3O4 content, the intensity of the peaks related to GQD-Fe3O4 increases, which confirms the successful synthesis of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites.

SEM images of CS (a,b), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5) (c,d), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) (e,f), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15) (g,h), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10)- chlorpyrifos (i,j) are shown in Fig. 2 . The left panel corresponds to 500 μm magnification of hydrogel beads, while the right panel to 10 μm magnification reveals the microstructural features of their surface. The hydrogel beads are spherical and had a rough and lacunaris surface. SEM image of GQD-Fe3O4 confirmed homogeneous spherical shapes on the layers of GQD sheets, which could be attributed to the attachment of Fe3O4 nanoparticles on the surface of GQD (Fig. S1). As can be seen in Fig. 2, the percentage of GQD-Fe3O4 had a large effect on the morphology of the hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites. The SEM image related to chitosan hydrogel beads is fibrous and rough with numerous pores resulting from an excellent swelling as intrinsic characteristics of the prepared hydrogel beads (Fig. 2 (a,b)). The addition of GQD-Fe3O4 to the matrix of chitosan caused important changes in the microstructure of the chitosan hydrogel beads, and GQD-Fe3O4 was formed in cubic structures. Therefore, by increasing the percentage of GQDS-Fe3O4 in the hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites, more cubic shapes are formed on the surface of chitosan, which confirmed the successful synthesis of the hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites (Fig. 2 c-h). In Fig. 2 (i,j), the adsorption of chlorpyrifos molecules by the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) (optimal adsorbent) is evident, which leads to the formation of a layer of chlorpyrifos molecules on the surface of the adsorbent) CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10)- Chlorpyrifos(.

Elemental analysis is utilized to measure the elements that compose a molecule or material. According to the results of the element analysis (Fig. 3 ), the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposite is composed of the components C, O, N, Na, and Fe. The adsorption of chlorpyrifos onto CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) gives rise to the peaks of chlorine (Cl), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P) in the EDS spectrum of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10)- chlorpyrifos.

The magnetic properties of the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15) were analyzed using the vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) technique (Fig. 4 ). The curves revealed an increase in the values of saturation magnetization with 0.87 emu/g, 2.59 emu/g, and 2.87 emu/g for CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15), respectively. This increase in saturation magnetization is attributed to the effect of increasing the GQD-Fe3O4 amount in the hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites. The CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites are superparamagnetic due to the absence of a hysteresis loop in which the remanence and coercivity are zero. The results showed that CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites can be easily separated from a solution by an external field.

Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis was performed to identify the surface characteristics of the adsorbent. According to Table 2 , the surface area, total pore volume (cm3/g), and total mean pore diameter (nm) of the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) nanocomposite were obtained as 17.56, 0. 28, and 14.55, respectively. The CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) nanocomposite is in the form of beads and in micrometric dimensions, and these characteristics are acceptable and appropriate compared to materials in these dimensions [36,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of pH on the swelling of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the swelling behaviors of the CS, CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15) were investigated in different pH solutions ranging from 3 to 11 as showed in Fig. 5 . The maximum swelling capacity of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites and chitosan was obtained at pH = 5, and the capacity was significantly reduced when pH was inclined to pH > 5 and pH < 5 range. In the pH < 5 regions, most of the carboxylate groups are protonated and converted from RCOO− (carboxylate) to RCOOH (carboxylic acid), thus reducing the anion-anion repulsive forces and thus reducing the swelling values. In the pH > 5, carboxyl groups on the hydrogel beads transform into negatively charged carboxylate ions, which causes more electrostatic repulsion. However, the swelling of hydrogel beads considerably decreased with rising pH at pH values >5. The reason for the reduced swelling may be due to the “charge shielding effect” of excess Na+ in the swelling solution, which shields the RCOO− anions and inhibits efficient electrostatic repulsion [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of GQD-Fe3O4 content on the swelling of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of GQD-Fe3O4 amount (5–15 %) on the swelling of hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 6 . The swelling ratio of the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites increases with increasing GQD-Fe3O4 amount until it reaches a maximum of 430.7 % at 10 %. This is because there are many hydrophilic functional groups, including –COOH and -OH on the GQD-Fe3O4 surface. This leads to a strong interaction (hydrogen bonding) of the hydrogel beads with water molecules and thus a higher adsorption capacity. However, after the GQD-Fe3O4 percentage was higher than 10 %, the swelling ratio was reduced. The explanation should be that high concentrations of GQD-Fe3O4 lead to denser network structures and prevent the penetration of water molecules into the networks of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites [38,39].

The advantages offered by the Response surface methodology (RSM) can be summarized as saving time and cost by reducing the number of experiments and determining the interaction between the operating parameters. Fig. 7 shows the Pareto chart results for the operational parameters. The Pareto chart shows the influencing factors (Only effective and statistically significant parameters) on the adsorption process from the largest effect to the smallest effect. In this study, all of the independent parameters (pH, time, concentration, and dosage) and their squares have statistically significant effects on the removal percentage. However, only the interaction effects related to pH and time are significant, and other interaction effects are not significant. As can be seen, the second-order effect of pH had the greatest effect and pH-time had the smallest effect on chlorpyrifos adsorption.

The experimental parameters were optimized to find the best conditions where all factors are optimal and the maximum removal efficiency is achieved. According to RSM optimization, the chlorpyrifos removal can be maximized at pH = 5, Time = 50, concentration = 30 ppm, and Dosage = 2 g/l. At these conditions, the adsorption of chlorpyrifos is theoretically 99.9972 % (Fig. 8 ). The results of pHZPC showed that at pH = 5, the surface charge of adsorbent and adsorbate are opposite, so one of the reasons for the suitability of this pH is the role of electrostatic attraction in the adsorption process. In general, as the contact time increases, the adsorption process becomes complete, but at a certain time, the process reaches equilibrium. In this study, the absorption process reached equilibrium in 50 min, and after that, increasing the time does not play a significant role in the progress of the adsorption process. In addition, as long as the adsorbent surface is not saturated by the pollutant, the absorption process will be complete if there is enough time, and the process has the highest efficiency. The results show that the adsorption process up to the pollutant concentration equal to 30 ppm is still being done with the highest efficiency. Also, increasing the amount of adsorbent increases the number of binding sites and thus makes the adsorption process better. But increasing the amount of adsorbent more than a certain amount (2 g/L in this study) increases the interactions of adsorbent particles with each other and thus does not play a role in increasing the efficiency of the process. Under similar conditions, the adsorption process was done and it was found that the adsorption of chlorpyrifos was 99.997 % with 0.0002 % deviation from the value predicted by the RSM model, which shows that RSM is a good method for modeling the process of chlorpyrifos adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The measurement of zero point of charge (ZPC)
ce:section.ce:para: The pH zero-point charge plays an important role in the adsorption process. When the pH of the solution is greater than pHzpc, the surface charge of the substance is negative and when the pH is less than pHzpc, the surface of the substance is positively charged. As shown in Fig. 9 , the pHZPC of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and chlorpyrifos is 5.88 and 6.88, respectively. The optimum value for initial pH is found to be 5. Therefore, it is proven that at pH = 5, the surface of the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) and chlorpyrifos are negatively and positively charged, respectively, which leads to electrostatic interaction between the negative sites on the adsorbent surface and the positively charged of chlorpyrifos.

There are various mechanisms such as hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, pore filling, π-π stacking, and hydrophobic interactions between CS/GQD-Fe3O4 and chlorpyrifos, as shown in Scheme 2 . One or more of these interactions are responsible for the adsorption of chlorpyrifos on the surface of the adsorbent. In most instances, π-π interactions played a major role in the adsorption process of pesticides [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GQD-Fe3O4 content on removal performance
ce:section.ce:para: The removal performance of CS, CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (5), CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10), and CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (15) nanocomposites towards chlorpyrifos pesticide under the optimal RSM condition are shown in Fig. 10 . The maximum adsorption efficiency of the pesticide chlorpyrifos was found at 10 % GQD-Fe3O4, which is a moderate proportion of GQD-Fe3O4. Above this ratio, the performance of chlorpyrifos pesticide removal decreases remarkably. This can be explained by swelling that high GQD-Fe3O4 concentrations create denser network structures and prevent the penetration of water molecules into the network of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites, which leads to a decrease in the adsorption process. In addition, it can be seen in the SEM images that the distribution of GQD–Fe3O4 nanocomposite on the surface of chitosan is homogeneous in low ratios. But at higher ratios of GQD-Fe3O4, it is heterogeneously distributed on the surface of chitosan, which leads to the blocking of numerous active sites and lowers the process' efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ultrasonic on removal performance
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the impact of ultrasonication on the removal efficiency, experiments were performed in the optimal conditions of RSM by varying the time from 5 min to 50 min (Fig. 11 ). The results showed that the removal of chlorpyrifos pesticide via ultrasonic-assisted method significantly reduces the equilibration time because the cavitation phenomenon improves the mass transfer through the convection path. Therefore, the ultrasonic technique can be used as an attractive advanced technology to increase the removal rate of pesticide contaminants in wastewater.

The study of adsorption equilibrium can provide mechanism information on the adsorption process, which is essential for designing optimal adsorption systems. The adsorption equilibrium study was performed under optimal conditions of RSM using twenty-one isotherm models. Non-linear equilibrium models of the one-parameter model (Henrys), two-parameter models (Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin, Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R), Halsey, Flory-Huggins, Elovich, Harkin-Jura, Jovanovic), three-parameter models (Frumkin (Fowler–Guggenheim), Hill, Toth, Sips, Redlich-Peterson, Khan, Jossens, Radke-Prausnitz), and four-parameter models (Fritz-Schlunder, Weber-Van Vliet, and Marczewski-Jaronic) were used to fit the experimental data. The parameters of the equilibrium models are shown in Table S1. According to Table S1, the value of RL in the range of 0.0097 < RL <0.896 (Langmuir), 1 < n (4.925) adsorption processes is favorable and physisorption. With respect to R2 (in descending order): Jossens > Weber-Van Vliet > Freundlich = Halsey = Hill = Redlich-Peterson = Toth = Sips = Fritz-Schlunder > Radke-Prausnitz > Flory-Huggins > Elovich > Temkin > Henrys > Khan > Langmuir > Marczewski-Jaronic > Frumkin > Jovanovic > Dubinin–Radushkevic > Harkin-Jura. According to the R2 values obtained from the isotherm models, the adsorption of chlorpyrifos on CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) fitted well with the Jossens isotherm model. The energy distribution of chlorpyrifos_CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) interactions in the active adsorption sites is the basis of the Jossens model. According to that model, chlorpyrifos adsorption on CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) has heterogeneous surfaces.

Adsorption kinetics is controlled by external diffusion, internal diffusion, and adsorption reaction on the active sites. The dynamic experimental data were evaluated using common nonlinear kinetic models. The simulation effects of the kinetic models for chlorpyrifos adsorption onto CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) are shown in Table S2 lists all the fitting parameters. The results showed that the Avrami model presents higher R2 for chlorpyrifos adsorption by CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10). Therefore, the adsorption rate coefficient has a temporal dependency during the adsorption process. In addition, the R2 of the double-exponential model is higher than other diffusion models (internal and external), so external and internal diffusion were considered. Also, internal and external diffusion affect the adsorption process of chlorpyrifos by CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) with different velocities (k1 < k2).

The temperature effect on chlorpyrifos adsorption by the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) was investigated at 25–55 °C (Table S3, Fig. 12 ). The thermodynamic parameters entropy (ΔS0), enthalpy (ΔH0), and free energy (ΔG0) are calculated using the Van't Hoff equation (Eqs. (17), (18), and (19)). where Ce is equilibrium concentration (mg/L), qe is the amount of chlorpyrifos adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent (mg/g), R is the ideal gas constant (8.314 J/(mol K)), m is the amount of composite (g/L), kc is the distribution coefficient, and T is the absolute temperature (°K). The ΔH° was found to be 227.309 kJ·mol−1, which is a negative value, thus revealing an exothermic reaction. The negative quantity of ΔS° showed a reduction in the chlorpyrifos concentration at the solid/solution interface during the adsorption process of chlorpyrifos on CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10). Also, ΔG° is negative for all temperatures, and thus this is a spontaneous reaction.
(17)
ln
k c
=
Δ
S o
R
−
Δ
H o
R
1 T
(18)
Δ
G o
= − RT ln
k c
(19)
k c
= m
q e
C e

Desorption of pesticide from the surface of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) in NaOH (0.1 M) solution was investigated. The reusability of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) in the removal of chlorpyrifos from aqueous solution through an adsorption-desorption process over 7 cycles was studied )Fig. 13 (. The results indicated that the efficient adsorption of chlorpyrifos was reduced from 99.76 % to 94.93 after 7 cycles, showing a minimum reduction of up to 4.8 %. This loss in efficiency could be due to numerous factors, including the saturation of active sites with pesticide molecules and damage to them at the adsorbent surface [41]. These results indicate that the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) can be reused to adsorb chlorpyrifos from aqueous solutions without significantly reducing the adsorption capacity and has high economic and performance potential for industrial application.

This study was carried out continuously for 2000 min under fixed conditions with effective parameters of bed height = 1.5 cm, initial concentration = 30 mg/L, and Q = 1.5 mL/min. The results of continuous adsorption experiments in FBAC were reported in Table 3 and shown in Fig. 14 . As can be seen, the breakthrough time (tb) and exhausting time (te) were equal to 485 min and 1099 min, respectively. Therefore, 2 g of this adsorbent can remove chlorpyrifos (30 ppm, 730 ml) from the wastewater in a continuous process for 8 h until its concentration reaches below the MRL (clean water). Also, the results showed that the adsorption percentage (%A) was equal to 24.72. As can be seen, the fixed-bed adsorption column saturates more slowly, and the bending nature of the breakthrough curve is good.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of pesticides from real wastewater samples
ce:section.ce:para: Agriculture wastewater and distilled water samples were used to assess the efficiency of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) nanocomposite synthesized in the chlorpyrifos removal from real wastewater. The treatment efficiency was performed in optimum conditions. As shown in Fig. 15 , the removal efficiency for the real wastewater samples decreased compared to the synthetic samples, which is probably ascribed to the existence of mineral species such as SO42−, Ca2+, K+, NO3−, Mg2+, etc. in agriculture wastewater. The high efficiency indicates that CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) nanocomposite has the ability to favorable the removal of pesticides from agriculture wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) nanocomposite with existing materials
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the equilibrium time (min) investigations, the equilibrium time of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) nanocomposite for chlorpyrifos is 10 min, and the rate of the process is more significant in comparison with existing materials for chlorpyrifos removal (Table 4 ). Furthermore, the results of investigating the effect of ultrasound waves on the removal performance showed that chlorpyrifos removal through the ultrasonic-assisted technique decreases the equilibrium time. Therefore, the CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) nanocomposite can be utilized as a fast and cost-effective adsorbent for chlorpyrifos removal.

In this study, chitosan-based hydrogel beads with different content of Fe3O4 and graphene quantum dots (CS/GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposites) were considered to investigate the effect of GQD-Fe3O4 content and ultrasonic on the removal performance of chlorpyrifos from aqueous environments. The characterization of the prepared hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites were confirmed using SEM, FT-IR, XRD, EDS-mapping, VSM, and BET studies. Investigating the effect of GQD-Fe3O4 content on the removal performance showed that a moderate proportion of GQD-Fe3O4 is suitable to obtain the maximum removal efficiency of the pesticide chlorpyrifos, i.e., at 10 % GQD-Fe3O4. The optimum chlorpyrifos adsorption (adsorption of 99.3 %) conditions were determined by the response surface method (initial concentration = 30 ppm, adsorbent = 2 g/l, pH = 5, time without ultrasonic = 50 min, and time with ultrasonic = 10 min). The investigation of the ultrasonic effect on the performance of removal showed that ultrasonic-assisted removal increases the rate of chlorpyrifos adsorption. The adsorption of chlorpyrifos on CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) adsorbent was well described by the Jossens, Avrami, and double exponential models and the results showed that the adsorption process was favorable, and physisorption. The study of the effect of pH on the swelling of hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites showed that the maximum swelling capacity of all hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites was obtained at pH = 5. The adsorption-desorption study showed the successful reuse of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) for chlorpyrifos adsorption in seven runs without a significant decrease (4.8 %) in adsorption efficiency. The results continuous adsorption experiment showed that the breakthrough time (tb) and exhausting time (te) of CS/GQD-Fe3O4 (10) were equal to 485 min and 1099 min, respectively. Therefore, the results of this study suggested that the prepared biosorbent can be useful and economically viable for wastewater treatment from organophosphate chlorpyrifos.
Mehdi Barzegarzadeh: Visualization, Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing- Original draft preparation.
Mohammad Sadegh Amini-Fazl: Supervision,Validation.
Negin Sohrab: Visualization, Investigation, Software.
No funding resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere gratitude to the University of Tabriz for providing financial support to this research.
